html:
Lord of the Flies — 10 Most Likely Questions

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How to use this guide

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). Memorise the structure once and the same shape carries every essay. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end and the same scene-bank answers all ten Qs.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on reader 2 — what the reader recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Golding may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

Lord of the Flies argues that civilisation is a thin layer over an inherited human capacity for cruelty — that without democratic institutions and reasoned dissent, group violence emerges in days, not generations, and the children of the most civilised nation are the most efficient at producing it. Golding writes against R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) — the Victorian myth that British boys placed in paradise will reproduce Christian civility — and offers in its place the painted hunters, the destroyed conch, and the cruiser belonging to the same adult war the boys had been fleeing. The boys carry every institutional inheritance of British public-school life onto the island and lose all of it within weeks; the cruiser at the close is the boys' tribe scaled up.

Key terms:

• Allegory-as-fable — an allegorical narrative whose surface story carries a clear didactic argument about how human institutions or human nature behave; Golding's own term for the novella in his 1965 essay Fable (collected in The Hot Gates).

• Symbolic object — a concrete object that carries an abstract institutional or moral idea; the conch (parliamentary procedure), the signal fire (institutional hope of rescue), Piggy's spectacles (reason), the painted face (institutional disinhibition), the Lord of the Flies (the inherited capacity for cruelty).

• Free-indirect prose — narrative technique that slips between third-person narration and the character's interior thought; the closing tricolon at Chapter 12 collapses Ralph's interior into Golding's authorial verdict.

• Deflationary irony — an ending that resolves the narrative tension in a register lower than the tension demanded; the naval officer's "Like the Coral Island" (Chapter 12) names the Victorian source text Golding has spent the novella refuting.

• Refused coming-of-age — a coming-of-age arc that does not reach integration; Ralph reaches Chapter 12 not as a matured leader but as hunted prey weeping for what has been lost, the form's most direct case.

---

Q1 — Ralph

Question

How does Golding present Ralph in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Ralph at this point in the novel

- how Golding presents Ralph in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Ralph is the institutional inheritance the novella destroys — democratically elected in Chapter 1, confused into recognition at Simon's killing, hunted as prey weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap. The plan tracks three stages of the same arc — Chapters 1–4 elected chief, Chapters 5–9 the chief who cannot hold the assembly, Chapters 10–12 the chief reduced to prey.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — surnames and Christian names chosen for symbolic meaning; Ralph inherited from Ballantyne's Coral Island and inverted.

• Free-indirect prose — narrative voice slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Symbolic object — a concrete object carrying an abstract institutional idea; the conch as parliamentary procedure.

• Tricolon — three parallel objects of grief in the closing sentence.

• Refused coming-of-age — a coming-of-age arc that ends in grief rather than integration.

• Deflationary irony — closing register that undercuts the dramatic stakes.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Golding chose the name Ralph directly from R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858), the Victorian boys' adventure novel in which Ralph and Jack reproduce Christian civility on a Pacific island and convert local people, a Sunday-school staple for generations of British children.

• Building sentence (historical context): Writing nine years after his Royal Navy service had taken him through the sinking of the Bismarck and the D-Day landings, Golding gave the same name to a boy whose institutional inheritance the novella destroys, the inversion designed to be unmissable to any reader carrying the Ballantyne expectation into the opening pages.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 election as the boy who carries the conch, his Chapter 9 dawning recognition that Simon's killing was murder, and his Chapter 12 weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap, Golding presents Ralph as the institutional inheritance the novella destroys — democratic election undone in twelve chapters by the inherited human capacity for cruelty the boys carried with them.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapters 1–4: elected chief (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the first assembly on the platform; Ralph elected chief because he carries the conch.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Ralph as the embodiment of British parliamentary inheritance — a boy elected not for charisma or ferocity but for the symbolic object he holds, the institution carried in his hands before he understands its weight.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys allegorical naming inside ritual procedure — Ralph announcing "I'll give the conch to the next person to speak. He can hold it when he's speaking" (Chapter 2) — combining allegorical naming (the conch elevated from beach-shell to parliamentary speaking-token, the institution made physical) with the modal-future construction ("can hold" extending the rule across all future assemblies, parliamentary procedure projected forward in time) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The single-clause imperative "He can hold it when he's speaking" makes parliamentary procedure available in object form, while the modal "can" converts a permission into a structural rule, the institution's discipline carried in a single twelve-year-old's voice.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the institutional optimism of the opening hour — a boy reaching instinctively for the parliamentary forms his school life has taught him — and feels the fragility of what is being installed.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the Ballantyne expectation being set up — the Victorian myth that British boys naturally reproduce civility — and registers Golding priming an inversion the rest of the novella will deliver.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Ralph's election to argue that institutional behaviour is learned rather than innate — the boys reproduce parliamentary form without yet understanding why it exists, the form itself the inheritance the novella will then test to destruction.

• Context (AO3): Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) had told Victorian boys that British boys on a Pacific island would reproduce Christian civility; the same name Ralph and the same setting are Golding's signal that his post-1945 reader is meant to feel the inversion as it lands.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–9: the chief who cannot hold the assembly (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the morning after Simon's killing on the beach; Ralph and Piggy unable to name what they have done.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had installed Ralph as the elected chief, Chapters 5–9 strip him to the chief who cannot hold the assembly together — institutional confidence collapsing into confused recognition that the boys he leads have killed the prophet who alone had seen the truth.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys free-indirect prose inside fragmented dialogue — Ralph's "That was Simon. […] That was murder" against Piggy's "It was an accident" (Chapter 10) — combining free-indirect prose (the narration slipping between Ralph's interior recognition and his halting speech, the reader granted access to the moment institutional confidence breaks) with declarative simplicity ("That was murder" monosyllabic and undefended, parliamentary register stripped to four blunt words) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The demonstrative "That" — repeated across both clauses — points back at the previous night's circle without naming it directly, while the noun "murder" converts the chant-driven killing into the legal-institutional category that the parliamentary forms of Chapter 2 had been designed to prevent.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the loss of institutional confidence as Ralph's own loss — the chief who had blown the conch in Chapter 2 reduced to a boy who can only point and name.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the moment institutional discipline falters — the recognition arriving after the cost, not before — and registers Golding's argument that institutions protect against the violence they themselves cannot survive.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Ralph's confused recognition to argue that institutional collapse is iterative rather than catastrophic — the elected chief who cannot name the killing has already lost the discipline that would have prevented it, the small failures of Chapters 5–8 enabling the larger failure of Chapter 9.

• Context (AO3): Golding had served in the Royal Navy from 1940 to 1945 and saw the Belsen liberation photographs in 1945; his 1959 remark that he had been "very impressed with what people are capable of doing" names the institutional collapse Ralph is here registering for the first time.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 10–12: hunted prey weeping (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the closing beach; Ralph weeping under the naval officer's white-topped cap as the cruiser stands offshore.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–9 had stripped Ralph to confused recognition, Chapters 10–12 complete the arc into refused coming-of-age — the elected chief reduced to hunted prey, the closing tricolon collapsing his interior into Golding's authorial verdict on the entire institutional descent.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys tricolon inside free-indirect prose — "Ralph wept for the end of innocence, the darkness of man's heart, and the fall through the air of the true, wise friend called Piggy" (Chapter 12) — combining tricolon (three parallel objects of grief building from the abstract "end of innocence" through the universal "darkness of man's heart" to the specific named friend "Piggy") with free-indirect prose (the narration fusing Ralph's grief with Golding's authorial verdict, the reader unable to separate boy from author) inside the same closing sentence.

• Close analysis: The verb "wept" — chosen against the more childlike "cried" — places Ralph in the elegiac register of adult mourning, while the adjective triplet "true, wise friend called Piggy" finally names the boy whose real name the institution had erased in Chapter 1, the institutional epithet unredeemed even at the close.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader experiences the closing sentence as the controlling concept landing in human form — the abstract institutional collapse and the concrete cost of one named friend converging on a single tricolon.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises Ralph as the protagonist of a refused coming-of-age — a boy whose maturation arrives only after irreparable loss, the recognition itself the form of the cost — and registers Golding's claim that the post-war reader is being asked to take the same journey in their own institutional life.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be addressing the post-war reader through Ralph's grief — the closing weeping not merely the protagonist's resolution but the reading the novella has been training, the Victorian myth of British civility refused at the cost the boys' island has charted.

• Context (AO3): John Carey's William Golding: The Man Who Wrote Lord of the Flies (2009) reads Ralph's closing weeping as Golding converting his own wartime experience into fable — the elected chief's grief for "the darkness of man's heart" the author's grief for the Belsen photographs given a twelve-year-old's voice.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding traces Ralph's journey from elected chief in Chapter 2 through confused recognition at Simon's killing in Chapter 9 to weeping prey under the naval officer's cap in Chapter 12 — the institutional inheritance the novella destroys made flesh in a single twelve-year-old's arc.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 installed as parliamentary inheritance, Chapters 5–9 fractured into confused recognition, Chapters 10–12 reduced to hunted grief — three stages of the same descent, the consistency of the destruction confirming Golding's claim that civilisation is a thin layer over an inherited capacity for cruelty.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1954 against the Victorian myth Ballantyne had encoded in 1858, Golding embodies in Ralph the institutional inheritance the post-war reader was being asked to defend consciously — the elected chief whose grief is the form's invitation to take the same journey before the cost is paid again.

• Ultimate moral: Ralph's enduring power as a fictional creation is that he is not heroic but inherited — the boy who carried the conch because his school life had taught him to, and whose weeping at the close is the institutional inheritance asking the reader to recognise what has been lost.

Q2 — Jack Merridew

Question

How does Golding present Jack in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Jack at this point in the novel

- how Golding presents Jack in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Jack is the anti-protagonist whose painted face permits the institutional inversion — head choirboy in Chapter 1, masked hunter in Chapter 4, charismatic tyrant of the painted tribe by Chapter 8. The plan tracks three stages of the same inversion — imperial confidence, masked disinhibition, tribal tyranny.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Jack inherited from Ballantyne's Coral Island and inverted; surname Merridew suggesting darkly ironic merry-doer.

• Symbolic object — the painted face as institutional disinhibition in object form.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Imperative chant — short rhythmic ritual speech ("Kill the pig").

• Antithesis — balanced opposites ("the mask was a thing on its own").

• Demonstrative pronoun (close analysis) — "behind which Jack hid".

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): In Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858), Jack is the bold, capable older boy who organises the three British castaways into Christian civility — and Golding chose the same name for the head choirboy whose painted face by Chapter 4 will lead the British public-school choir into the painted hunters of Chapter 8.

• Building sentence (historical context): Golding had taught at Bishop Wordsworth's School in Salisbury from 1945 to 1962 and drafted the novella while watching schoolboys in person; the choir's "black caps" and "long black cloaks" of Chapter 1 are the British public-school plus Anglican-chapel inheritance the novella will trace into the painted tribe by Chapter 12.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 2 imperial confidence in declaring "We're English; and the English are best at everything", his Chapter 4 discovery that the painted face liberates him from shame, and his Chapter 8 walking out of the assembly to found the painted tribe at Castle Rock, Golding presents Jack as the anti-protagonist whose institutional inversion permits the inherited capacity for cruelty to be acted out under the cover of charismatic ritual.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapters 1–4: imperial confidence to masked disinhibition (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Jack's Chapter 2 "We're English" speech, then Jack painting his face in Chapter 4.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Jack as the voice of imperial confidence and then strips that voice to its underlying disinhibition — the institutional inversion arriving the moment Jack discovers that the painted face permits what the unpainted face had still found shameful.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys antithesis inside free-indirect prose — Jack's Chapter 2 "We've got to have rules and obey them. After all, we're not savages. We're English; and the English are best at everything" set against the Chapter 4 narration that "the mask was a thing on its own, behind which Jack hid, liberated from shame and self-consciousness" — combining antithesis (the imperial-civility speech of Chapter 2 set against the masked-disinhibition discovery of Chapter 4, two incompatible Jacks within two chapters) with personification (the mask granted independent agency as "a thing on its own", the institution given a voice that is not Jack's) inside the same arc.

• Close analysis: The relative clause "behind which Jack hid" makes the mask the agent and Jack the hidden subject — a grammatical inversion that mirrors the institutional inversion the painting performs — while "liberated from shame and self-consciousness" names the institutional inhibitions the mask removes.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the imperial-civility claim of Chapter 2 collapse two chapters later into the masked-hunter discovery — the speed of the inversion the novella's first formal warning that institutional discipline is not deep.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the painted face as institutional disinhibition in object form — the mechanism by which inherited cruelty bypasses inherited inhibition — and registers Golding's argument that the choir was always already capable of the hunt.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the painted-face discovery to argue that institutional inhibition is the only barrier between inherited capacity and acted-out cruelty — Jack's "liberated" is the novella's bleakest single verb, naming the institution as the discipline that liberation discards.

• Context (AO3): Golding's Chapter 1 description of the choir "marching approximately in step in two parallel lines" with "black caps" and "long black cloaks" is the British public-school plus Anglican-chapel tradition arriving in uniform — the institution Golding will spend the novella tracing into the painted tribe.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–9: walking out and founding the tribe (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Jack walking out of Ralph's assembly in Chapter 8 and gathering the boys into the painted tribe.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had installed Jack's discovery of masked disinhibition, Chapters 5–9 stage the moment that discovery becomes institutional act — Jack walking out of Ralph's assembly to found the painted tribe at Castle Rock, the institutional inversion completed at the level of the group.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys declarative simplicity inside structural rupture — Jack's Chapter 8 "I'm not going to play any longer. Not with you" — combining declarative simplicity (the monosyllabic refusal stripped of qualification, the parliamentary register Ralph had carried in Chapter 2 explicitly rejected) with structural rupture (the assembly broken by a single walking-out, the conch left on the platform without authority to recall the absent boys) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The verb "play" domesticates the parliamentary process to childhood game — converting Ralph's institutional inheritance into something Jack can simply leave — while the elided second sentence "Not with you" makes Ralph alone the rejected institution, the personal frame masking the institutional refusal.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional fracture as a single sentence that the conch cannot answer — parliamentary procedure undone by a refusal it has no mechanism to recall.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises charismatic tyranny choosing its moment — the institutional inversion not imposed by force but elected by the group who follow Jack out of the assembly — and registers Golding's argument that institutions die not by overthrow but by abandonment.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the walk-out to argue that institutional collapse is permissive rather than violent in its first stage — Jack's tribe forms because no procedure compels the boys to stay, the absence of compulsion the institutional weakness that the painted face will then exploit.

• Context (AO3): R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) had let its Jack lead the three boys into Christian civility; Golding's Jack walking out of the assembly to found the painted tribe is the Ballantyne arc inverted at the structural pivot of the novella.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 10–12: tribal tyranny and the burning island (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: Jack's tribe at Castle Rock; Roger at the boulder; the manhunt for Ralph; the burning island.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–9 had founded the painted tribe, Chapters 10–12 confirm Jack as charismatic tyrant of an institutional order that no longer requires his discovery to function — the painted face now the rule rather than the exception, the burning island the tribe's signature scaled to the geography itself.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys the imperative chant inside ritualised tribal speech — "Kill the beast! Cut his throat! Spill his blood!" (Chapter 9, repeated through Chapters 10–12) — combining the imperative chant (the trochaic stresses and monosyllabic verbs stripping speech of the procedural inhibitions the conch had carried) with parallelism (the three-line repetition installing ritual rhythm that the parliamentary debate of Chapter 2 had been designed to prevent) inside the same recurring chorus.

• Close analysis: The verbs "Kill", "Cut", "Spill" — three monosyllabic imperatives stacked in escalating violence — strip language of qualification, while the noun "beast" slides between the dead parachutist, the imagined external monster, and the boys themselves, the institutional projection operating in three registers at once.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the chant as institutional disinhibition in linguistic form — the conch's opposite, speech without procedure, voice without rotation, mass without restraint.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises charismatic tyranny as the institutional form the painted tribe has elected — the choir's hymn replaced by the hunt's chant — and registers Golding's claim that the chapel and the hunt were never as far apart as the Victorian myth supposed.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the manhunt and the burning island to argue that institutional tyranny is self-amplifying once disinhibition has been ritualised — Jack's tribe burning the whole island to flush Ralph out is the inherited capacity for cruelty operating without the discipline that would have contained it.

• Context (AO3): Golding's own essay Fable (in The Hot Gates, 1965) names the institutional argument the novella was making and describes his wartime service as the experience that broke his pre-war optimism about humanity — Jack's painted tribe the institutional argument given twelve-year-old form.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding constructs Jack as the anti-protagonist whose institutional inversion permits the inherited capacity for cruelty to be acted out under charismatic cover — imperial confidence in Chapter 2, masked disinhibition in Chapter 4, tribal tyranny by Chapter 12.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 installed as imperial-civility claim, Chapters 5–9 acted on as institutional walk-out, Chapters 10–12 ritualised as tribal chant — three stages of the same inversion, the painted face the institutional discipline that liberation discards.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1954 reader still confronting the Belsen photographs, Golding embodies in Jack the institutional inversion he had watched adult institutions produce on a continental scale — the head choirboy whose painted face is the inherited capacity for cruelty given a twelve-year-old's institutional permission slip.

• Ultimate moral: Jack's enduring power is that he is not invented but observed — the institutional discipline that "liberated from shame and self-consciousness" is the institutional warning the post-war reader is being asked to recognise in their own institutional life before the painting begins again.

Q3 — Piggy

Question

How does Golding use Piggy to suggest ideas about reason and society in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Piggy at this point in the novel

- how Golding uses Piggy to suggest ideas about reason and society.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Piggy is reason embodied in a boy the institution has taught the others to mock — gatekeeper of parliamentary procedure in Chapters 1–4, increasingly desperate keeper of the conch through Chapters 5–9, killed at the moment he tries to reassert procedure at Castle Rock. The plan tracks three stages of the same disabling.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Piggy as institutional epithet replacing a real name; the original name never given.

• Symbolic object — the conch as parliamentary procedure; the spectacles as reason in object form.

• Rhetorical question — Piggy's interrogative challenges to the tribe.

• Free-indirect prose — narration slipping into the character's interior thought.

• Tricolon — three parallel imperatives or claims.

• Single-clause verb (close analysis) — "exploded".

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 115 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Golding never tells the reader Piggy's real name — the institutional epithet imposed in the opening pages by the other boys, with Ralph's failed defence "He's not Fatty,' cried Ralph, 'his real name's Piggy!" (Chapter 1), survives the entire novella and is still the only name in Ralph's closing tricolon.

• Building sentence (historical context): Piggy arrives in the opening pages with asthma, spectacles, plump build, and a working-class accent — every marker the British public-school tradition would set against him — and Golding constructs him as the institutional voice of reason in a body the inherited social architecture has already taught the other boys to mock.

• Thesis: Across his Chapter 1 first identification of the conch and his suggestion of the meeting, his Chapter 11 final challenge "Which is better — to have rules and agree, or to hunt and kill?", and his death at Castle Rock in the same moment as the conch's destruction, Golding uses Piggy to argue that reason in this society is not innate strength but institutionally disabled — the voice that should be heard is the voice the institution has trained the others to ignore.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 — Chapters 1–4: gatekeeper of procedure, voice the institution mocks (7 sentences — about 220 words)

Scene focus: the opening pages; Piggy identifying the conch, suggesting the meeting, calling the roll, and being mocked.

• Topic sentence: In the opening chapters, Golding installs Piggy as the gatekeeper of parliamentary procedure — the boy who first identifies the conch and suggests the assembly — and the institutional disabling of reason as the corresponding inheritance, the voice that should be heard already trained out of the others' attention.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys allegorical naming inside ritual disabling — Ralph's failed defence "He's not Fatty,' cried Ralph, 'his real name's Piggy!" (Chapter 1) immediately followed by the boys laughing — combining allegorical naming (the institutional epithet imposed by the group, the original name erased before the reader is given it) with structural irony (the boy who identifies the conch and reasons about the fire is the boy whose name the elected chief cannot defend) inside the same opening sequence.

• Close analysis: The reported verb "cried" — chosen against the more controlled "said" — registers Ralph's institutional weakness in real time, while the bare possessive "his real name" names a name the novella refuses to deliver, the institutional erasure carried in the absence the reader is left with.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader feels the institutional disabling of reason in the same scene as its identification — the boy whose voice should carry the parliamentary inheritance is the boy whose name the institution will never restore.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the cultural disabling of reason — the way inherited social architecture teaches a group to mock the very voice it most needs to hear — and registers Golding's claim that the institutional inheritance the boys carry already contains the seed of its own collapse.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the naming scene to argue that reason in this society is not naturally disabled but institutionally disabled — the others have been trained to mock Piggy by the same school life that taught them to elect Ralph, the inheritance bringing both the discipline and its disabling.

• Context (AO3): Golding had taught at Bishop Wordsworth's School in Salisbury from 1945 to 1962 and watched the British public-school inheritance in person; Piggy's mockery is the institutional disabling of reason Golding had observed in the inherited social architecture itself.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 — Chapters 5–9: increasingly desperate keeper of the conch (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: the broken assembly in Chapter 5; Piggy's increasingly desperate appeals to procedure as the tribe forms.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 1–4 had installed Piggy as gatekeeper of procedure, Chapters 5–9 stage the increasing desperation of his appeals — the conch's authority eroding as the painted tribe forms, reason still articulate but no longer institutionally heard.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys rhetorical question inside parliamentary appeal — Piggy's recurring "Which is better — to have rules and agree, or to hunt and kill?" (Chapter 11, anticipated through Chapters 5–9) — combining rhetorical question (the parliamentary form preserved even as the audience for it dissolves, reason's syntax surviving its institutional disabling) with antithesis ("rules and agree" set against "hunt and kill", the parliamentary inheritance set against the painted tribe's chosen alternative) inside the same recurring challenge.

• Close analysis: The interrogative form — "Which is better" — refuses to assert and instead invites the boys to choose, parliamentary procedure preserved even at the moment of its disabling, while the gerunds "to agree" and "to kill" convert two abstract nouns into two visible institutional acts.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears reason still articulate even as the institution that should have carried it dissolves — Piggy's challenge a parliamentary speech delivered to an audience that has already left the chamber.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises reason's institutional disabling as the precondition for the painted tribe's success — the voice that asks "which is better" still posing the right question, but to a group that has stopped answering — and registers Golding's argument that institutional disabling silences reason long before reason itself is killed.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using Piggy's increasingly desperate questions to argue that reason without institutional protection is rhetorical only — the parliamentary form survives its disabling but cannot reverse it, the asking itself no longer institutionally consequential.

• Context (AO3): The British public-school plus Anglican-chapel tradition taught parliamentary debate as a civic skill; Piggy's preserved rhetorical question is the inherited skill carried into a chamber that has elected to walk out, the institutional disabling visible in the absence of any audience to address.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 — Chapters 10–12: killed with the conch (7 sentences — about 230 words)

Scene focus: Castle Rock in Chapter 11; Roger at the lever; the boulder; the conch and Piggy destroyed in the same moment.

• Topic sentence: Where Chapters 5–9 had made Piggy's appeals increasingly desperate, Chapter 11 stages the institutional argument made physical — Roger's boulder destroying both Piggy and the conch in the same moment, reason and parliamentary procedure killed in a single descending stone.

• Technique + evidence: Golding deploys symbolic object inside single-clause description — "the conch exploded into a thousand white fragments and ceased to exist" (Chapter 11) — combining symbolic object (the conch's destruction the institutional collapse rendered in physical form, parliamentary procedure shattered in one verb) with the absolute final clause ("ceased to exist" refusing any institutional remainder, the conch not merely broken but ontologically removed) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The single-clause verb "exploded" combines the destruction of reason and parliamentary procedure into one event — Piggy and the conch reduced to debris in the same moment — while "a thousand white fragments" converts the institutional whole into countable rubble that no boy on the island can reassemble.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears the destruction of reason as the destruction of an institution — Piggy's death and the conch's shattering registered as one event by the form itself.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the institutional argument made physical — the moment the inherited capacity for cruelty has unmade the inherited capacity for reasoned dissent in the same sentence — and registers Golding's claim that reason without institutional defence is killed in the same descent that kills the institution.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the simultaneous destruction to argue that reason and parliamentary procedure are not separate inheritances but a single institutional architecture — when the conch is destroyed, Piggy is destroyed, because reason in this society has no shelter outside the institution that protected it.

• Context (AO3): Golding's Belsen-photograph generation had watched institutional violence on a continental scale; the simultaneous destruction of Piggy and the conch is the institutional argument the post-war reader is being asked to take into their own institutional life — reason disabled, then killed, in the same descent.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 145 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding uses Piggy to anatomise reason in this society as institutionally disabled rather than naturally weak — gatekeeper mocked in Chapter 1, increasingly desperate questioner through Chapters 5–9, killed with the conch at Castle Rock in Chapter 11.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 installed as institutional disabling, Chapters 5–9 left as preserved rhetorical form, Chapter 11 destroyed in a single boulder — three stages of the same disabling, reason and parliamentary procedure killed together because they had always been a single institutional architecture.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1954, Golding embodies in Piggy the institutional disabling of reason he had watched the inherited social architecture produce — the boy whose real name is never given the institutional warning that the post-war reader is being asked to register before the next descent begins.

• Ultimate moral: Piggy's enduring power is that he is not the voice of weakness but the voice of disabling — reason articulate to the end, killed not by inferior argument but by an institutional architecture that elected to stop listening.

Q4 — Simon

Question

How does Golding present Simon as a different kind of boy in Lord of the Flies?

Write about:

- how Golding presents Simon at this point in the novel

- how Golding presents Simon in the novel as a whole.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Simon is the secondary protagonist whose moral insight the institution does not survive — quiet watcher in Chapters 1–4, prophet hallucinating the Lord of the Flies in Chapter 8, killed by the chant before he can deliver his recognition. The plan tracks three stages — withdrawal, vision, killed messenger.

Technique gloss:

• Allegorical naming — Simon as the apostle name in the Christian tradition, the name Christ gave Peter.

Q5 — Civilisation versus Savagery

Question

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Golding presents the conflict between civilisation and savagery.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame

Civilisation versus savagery is the novella's defining thematic opposition — civilisation carried as institutional inheritance in Chapters 1–4, fractured by the painted tribe and Simon's killing in Chapters 5–9, destroyed at Castle Rock and reframed at scale by the cruiser in Chapters 10–12. The plan tracks three stages of the same opposition, ending with the deflationary widening to the adult war.

Technique gloss:

• Allegory-as-fable — narrative whose surface story carries didactic argument about institutions.

• Symbolic object — the conch (parliamentary procedure), the painted face (institutional disinhibition).

• Antithesis — balanced opposites (Ralph's election against Jack's painted tribe).

• Imperative chant — short rhythmic ritual speech ("Kill the pig").

• Deflationary irony — closing register that undercuts the dramatic stakes.

• Demonstrative pronoun (close analysis) — the naval officer's "you're all British, aren't you?".

• Hook (historical fact): Golding finished Lord of the Flies in 1953 and saw it rejected by twenty-one publishers before Faber accepted it in 1954, nine years after he had returned from Royal Navy service that had taken him through the sinking of the Bismarck, the D-Day landings, and exposure to the Belsen liberation photographs of 1945.

Scene focus: the Chapter 2 assembly; the conch installed as parliamentary token; Jack's "We're English" speech.

• Effect on reader 1: Thus the reader hears civilisation as inherited rather than innate — the boys reaching for parliamentary forms because their school life has trained them to, the discipline visibly carried rather than visibly chosen.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises the Ballantyne expectation being primed for inversion — Jack's "the English are best at everything" the Victorian myth voiced by a head choirboy the novella will paint by Chapter 4 — and registers Golding's argument that the inheritance contains both the discipline and the assumption that the discipline is automatic.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the parallel between Ralph's procedural rule and Jack's imperial-civility claim to argue that civilisation in this society is two inheritances at once — the institutional discipline that holds the assembly and the imperial myth that the discipline does not need to be defended.

• Context (AO3): Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) had told Victorian boys that British boys on a Pacific island would naturally reproduce Christian civility; the Chapter 2 assembly is Golding's setup for the inversion, Ralph's conch carried into the scene whose painted tribe will refute Ballantyne by Chapter 8.

• Effect on reader 2: Indeed, the reader recognises that the rescuing institution belongs to the same adult war the boys had been fleeing — the institution that destroys the conch's brother on a planetary scale the institution that arrives to rescue what little remains — and registers Golding's claim that the boys' violence and the adult war's violence are not different in kind, only in scale.

• Author's purpose: Golding may be using the deflationary closing to argue that the conflict between civilisation and savagery is not resolved by rescue but only relocated — the boys saved by the institution whose larger violence created their plane crash, the post-war reader asked to recognise that the Victorian myth has been refuted at both scales at once.

• Context (AO3): John Carey's William Golding: The Man Who Wrote Lord of the Flies (2009) reads the cruiser's arrival as the novella's most carefully prepared inversion — the institution that rescues belonging to the same war that produced the crash, the imperial-civility myth refuted by the very form of the closing scene.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 150 words)

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Golding stages civilisation versus savagery as the same anthropology at two scales — institutional inheritance in Chapters 1–4, counter-institutional tribe in Chapters 5–9, deflationary cruiser in Chapters 10–12 — the conflict resolved only on the surface by a rescue that belongs to the larger version of the same violence.

• Controlling concept: What Chapters 1–4 installed as parliamentary inheritance, Chapters 5–9 fractured into chanting tribe, Chapters 10–12 widened to deflationary cruiser — three operations of the same opposition, civilisation revealed as a thin layer over an inherited capacity for cruelty operating at every scale at once.

• Author's purpose: Writing in 1954 against the 1858 Victorian myth, Golding offers an ultimatum to two assumptions — to the Victorian assumption that British boys are naturally civilised, the painted face and Roger's boulder; to the post-war assumption that civilisation has been restored by the Allied victory of 1945, the cruiser belonging to the same Cold War the boys had been fleeing.

• Ultimate moral: The novella's deepest claim is that civilisation is institutional rather than innate, fragile rather than restored, and the post-war reader closing the book is the protagonist of the third chapter in the larger institutional descent the cruiser at the close has not, in fact, prevented.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 themes

Every essay should thread one of these into one BP. Pick the factor whose period-fit is sharpest for the Q.

1. WWII + Golding's Royal Navy service 1940–45 + the Belsen liberation photographs (1945). Best fit: Q1 Ralph, Q3 Piggy, Q4 Simon, Q5 Civilisation vs Savagery, Q7 Fear and the Beast, Q8 Loss of Innocence, Q10 Good and Evil. Why it lands: Golding's 1959 remark "I was very impressed with what people are capable of doing" names the institutional collapse the boys' island miniaturises; the painted tribe and Simon's killing are the institutional pattern Golding had observed adult institutions produce on a continental scale.

2. The Cold War + the nuclear arms race + the H-bomb tests (US 1952 / USSR 1953 / UK 1957). Best fit: Q5 Civilisation vs Savagery, Q6 Power and Leadership, Q7 Fear and the Beast, Q9 Symbolism. Why it lands: the cruiser at the close belongs to the same Cold War institutional order the boys' plane crash was fleeing; the institutional argument scales from boys to adults in the same anthropology operating at two scales at once.

3. R.M. Ballantyne's The Coral Island (1858) + the Victorian myth of British boys. Best fit: Q1 Ralph, Q2 Jack, Q5 Civilisation vs Savagery, Q6 Power and Leadership, Q8 Loss of Innocence, Q10 Good and Evil. Why it lands: Ballantyne's three British boys (Ralph, Jack, Peterkin) reproduce Christian civility on the Pacific island; Golding chose the same names and the same setting to make the inversion unmissable, and the naval officer's "Like the Coral Island" (Chapter 12) names the source text the novella has spent its descent refuting.

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 themes

Every essay should name one critic, with year, in one BP or in the conclusion. Pick the voice whose claim is sharpest for the Q.

1. John Carey, William Golding: The Man Who Wrote Lord of the Flies (2009). The authoritative biography reads Golding's career-long institutional argument as continuous with his Royal Navy service — argues that Lord of the Flies is the dramatic distillation of Golding's wartime experience converted into post-war fable, the closing tricolon the elegiac registration of the institutional collapse the Belsen photographs had asked the post-war reader to register. Best fit: Q1 Ralph (the elegiac arc), Q4 Simon (the prophet killed), Q5 Civilisation vs Savagery (the deflationary cruiser), Q7 Fear and the Beast (the institutional pattern), Q8 Loss of Innocence (the closing tricolon), Q10 Good and Evil (the elegiac verdict).

2. Stephen Medcalf, William Golding (Writers and Their Work, 1975). Reads the novella as a religious-allegorical fable in the tradition of Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress (1678) and Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) — argues that the symbolic objects (the conch, the painted face, the Lord of the Flies) deliver the institutional argument the realist surface alone could not authorise, and that the religious-allegorical naming (Simon as apostle, Lord of the Flies as Beelzebub) locates the argument in the post-war fable tradition's chief argumentative inheritance. Best fit: Q2 Jack (the painted face), Q3 Piggy (reason institutionally disabled), Q4 Simon (religious-allegorical naming), Q6 Power and Leadership (the institutional dialectic), Q9 Symbolism (the form's chief technical move), Q10 Good and Evil (religious-allegorical recognition).
